---
title: Expo / React Native
description: Mobile SQLite dialects (expo-sqlite, Nitro SQLite) and setup notes for offline-first apps
---

# Expo / React Native

Mobile users expect apps to work offline. Syncular’s “local SQLite is the primary read + write path” model maps well to React Native.

## Recommended dialects

- **Expo managed workflow:** `@syncular/dialect-expo-sqlite`
- **React Native (Nitro SQLite):** `@syncular/dialect-react-native-nitro-sqlite`

## Minimal setup (expo-sqlite)

```ts
import { openDatabaseSync } from 'expo-sqlite';
import { createDatabase } from '@syncular/core';
import { createExpoSqliteDialect } from '@syncular/dialect-expo-sqlite';
import { ensureClientSyncSchema } from '@syncular/client';

const db = createDatabase({
  dialect: createExpoSqliteDialect({ name: 'app.db', openDatabaseSync }),
  family: 'sqlite',
});
await ensureClientSyncSchema(db);
```

## Known-good mobile sync setup

Use the React Native HTTP transport preset instead of manually tuning
transport capabilities:

```ts
import { createReactNativeHttpTransport } from '@syncular/transport-http';

const transport = createReactNativeHttpTransport({
  baseUrl: 'https://api.example.com',
  getHeaders: async () => ({ Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` }),
});
```

That preset enables the runtime behavior mobile apps usually need:

- byte-based snapshot chunk fetches instead of streaming assumptions
- buffered gzip fallback when `DecompressionStream` is unavailable
- per-subscription bootstrap commits so one heavy subscription does not block
  lighter ones
- materialized snapshot application preferred for Hermes / Expo runtimes

## Recommended bootstrap pattern

Large mobile datasets should not all block first render.

Use `bootstrapPhase` on subscriptions:

- phase `0`: the minimum data needed to render initial UI
- phase `1+`: larger or relation-heavy datasets that can load afterward

Example:

```ts
sync.addHandler({
  table: 'catalog_meta',
  scopes: ['public'],
  subscribe: () => ({
    id: 'catalog-meta',
    table: 'catalog_meta',
    scopes: {},
    bootstrapPhase: 0,
  }),
});

sync.addHandler({
  table: 'code_relations',
  scopes: ['public'],
  subscribe: () => ({
    id: 'catalog-relations',
    table: 'code_relations',
    scopes: {},
    bootstrapPhase: 1,
  }),
});
```

In React, use `useSyncBootstrapState()` from `@syncular/client-react` instead
of reading sync tables directly:

```tsx
const bootstrap = useSyncBootstrapState();

if (bootstrap.pendingBlocking.length > 0) {
  return <LoadingScreen />;
}
```

This keeps startup gating tied to the actual active bootstrap phase instead of
guessing from local SQLite state.

## Practical guidance

- Prefer `expo-sqlite` unless you already need a different native SQLite stack.
- Keep the initial mobile bootstrap small; move relation-heavy tables into later
  phases.
- Treat the local SQLite DB as the source of truth for reads. Sync should fill
  it in the background.
- If you test local mobile clients against a remote server, make sure the server
  CORS/origin policy explicitly allows loopback or device origins.
